{{Use mdy dates|date=August 2025}}

| Nickname           = ''El Huracán Azul''<br>(The Blue Hurricane)<br>''Los Boricuas''<br>(The Boricuas)

| FIFA min date      = November 2004

| Elo min            = 201
| Elo min date       = February 3, 2001; July 21, 2002

| socks2             = ffffff
<!--End of Shirt-->
| First game         = {{fb|CUB|1902}} 1–1 {{fb-rt|PUR|1912}}<br>([[Havana]], Cuba; November 12, 1940)
| Largest win        = {{fb|PUR}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|SMT}}<br>([[Port-au-Prince]], Haiti; September 9, 2012)
| Largest loss       = {{nowrap|{{fb|ANT}} 15–0 {{fb-rt|PUR|1952}}}}<br>([[Caracas]], Venezuela; January 15, 1959)
| Regional name      = 
| Regional cup apps  = 
| Regional cup first = 
| Regional cup best  = 
}}

The '''Puerto Rico national football team''' ({{langx|es|Selección de fútbol de Puerto Rico}}) represents [[Puerto Rico]] in men's international [[Association football|football]], and are governed by the [[Federación Puertorriqueña de Fútbol]] ({{langx|en|Puerto Rican Football Federation}}). The team's nickname is ''El Huracán Azul'' meaning The Blue Hurricane.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.elvocero.com/deportes/se-rejuvenece-el-hurac-n-azul/article_766dee88-c927-11e9-b69d-5306f224e1a5.html|title=Se rejuvenece el Huracán Azul|author=Yamaira Muñiz Pérez |website=El Vocero de Puerto Rico|date=August 28, 2019 |access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=August 28, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190828150957/https://www.elvocero.com/deportes/se-rejuvenece-el-hurac-n-azul/article_766dee88-c927-11e9-b69d-5306f224e1a5.html|url-status=live}}</ref> They are members of the [[Caribbean Football Union]], and part of [[CONCACAF]]. The team has never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] or the [[CONCACAF Gold Cup]].

== History ==
=== Early years (1940s–1980s) ===
Puerto Rico played its first international match against [[Cuba national football team|Cuba]] on November 12, 1940, a game that ended in a 1-1 draw. Six years later, it participated in the [[1946 Central American and Caribbean Games]] in [[Barranquilla]], [[Colombia]], its first official competition. It finished in last place, suffering some embarrassing defeats, starting with a 12-0 loss to [[Costa Rica national football team|Costa Rica]]. Its next five games ended in defeat: 12-1 against [[Panama national football team|Panama]], 4-1 against the host country, [[Colombia national football team|Colombia]], 4-1 against [[Guatemala national football team|Guatemala]], 14-0 against the Antilles, and finally, on December 26, 1946, it closed its participation with a 6-0 loss to [[Venezuela national football team|Venezuela]]. In 1959, it returned to compete in the 8th edition of the regional games in [[Caracas]]. It improved slightly on its previous performance, but this was not enough to avoid finishing last, ultimately securing fifth place.

Between 1959 and 1986, Puerto Rico was a regular participant in the Central American and Caribbean Games, competing in every edition without interruption.

They did not record a win until a 3–0 result in 1970 against [[Bahamas national football team|the Bahamas]]. In 1972, Puerto Rico recorded a 1–0 win against [[Panama national football team|Panama]] in a [[Exhibition game|friendly]]. The 1970s saw Puerto Rico participate in its first [[FIFA World Cup qualifiers|World Cup qualifier]], specifically for the [[1974 FIFA World Cup]]. They were eliminated by [[Haiti national football team|Haiti]], the eventual continental champions, who won with a resounding 12-0 aggregate score. Puerto Rico would not play in another qualifier until 1986. In the meantime, they hosted and participated in the [[1979 Pan American Games]] in [[San Juan, Puerto Rico|San Juan]]. Although they advanced from the group stage, they were unable to progress beyond the second round and finished in 5th place after the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]] withdrew. They also hosted the [[1981 CFU Championship]], though they finished fourth and last in the final round.<ref>{{Cite web |title=CFU Championship 1981 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/cfu81.html |access-date=2026-02-09 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

Puerto Rico returned to the preliminary rounds of the FIFA World Cup for the [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification|1986 World Cup qualifiers]], where they were eliminated in a two-legged tie by [[El Salvador national football team|El Salvador]], who won 8-0 on aggregate. Also, at the [[1986 Central American and Caribbean Games|1986 edition]] in [[Santiago de los Caballeros]], [[Dominican Republic]], they reached the quarterfinals, losing 4-0 to an amateur team from [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Central American and Caribbean Games 1986 (Dominican Republic) |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesd/domrep-tourn86.html |access-date=2026-02-09 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

Four years later they were eliminated again in the preliminary round of the [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification|1990 World Cup qualifiers]], although [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] prevailed by a less lopsided aggregate score of 3:1. Puerto Rican wins between the 1980s and 1990s were mainly against the [[Bahamas national football team|Bahamas]], the [[Dominican Republic national football team|Dominican Republic]], and [[Martinique national football team|Martinique]].<ref name="rsssf.com">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/prico-intres.html |title=Puerto Rico&nbsp;– List of International Matches |website=[[RSSSF]] |publisher=Rec Sport Soccer Statistics Foundation |date=January 31, 2007 |access-date=June 30, 2010 |archive-date=March 25, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230325083425/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/prico-intres.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== 1990s ===
The most significant event of the 1990s for the Puerto Rico team was their qualification for the final stage of the [[Caribbean Cup]] in [[Jamaica]] in 1993. Drawn in the same group as the host nation, Jamaica, along with [[Saint Kitts and Nevis national football team|Saint Kitts and Nevis]] and [[Sint Maarten national football team|Sint Maarten]], Puerto Rico got off to a strong start, defeating Sint Maarten 3-0. Unfortunately, they lost twice by a single goal to their other two opponents, losing any chance of reaching the tournament semifinals. Puerto Rico has not yet managed to return to the group stage of the Caribbean Cup.

[[Chris Armas]] played for Puerto Rico in the [[1993 Caribbean Cup]]. In the tournament, Puerto Rico established a 4-game win streak without conceding a goal.<ref name="rsssf.com" /> The competition was not then recognized by [[FIFA]], and so his five matches were considered [[Exhibition game|friendlies]]. As a result, he was later allowed to switch his allegiance to the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0FCN/is_6_23/ai_69057380 |date = February 2001|access-date=December 10, 2007 |title=Now You See Him |work=Soccer Digest |first=Michael  |last=Lewis  |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20071221001642/http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0FCN/is_6_23/ai_69057380 <!-- Bot retrieved archive --> |archive-date =December 21, 2007}}</ref> He would later receive 66 caps for the United States.{{citation needed|date=June 2020}}

In the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification|1994 World Cup qualifiers]], Puerto Rico confirmed its progress, defeating the Dominican Republic in the first round (3-2 on aggregate) with a memorable 1-2 victory in Santo Domingo on March 21, 1992. In the following round, they were eliminated again by Jamaica, who won 3-1 on aggregate. In the [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification|1998 World Cup qualifiers]], they failed to replicate their previous success, suffering a crushing 9-1 defeat to [[Saint Vincent and the Grenadines national football team|Saint Vincent and the Grenadines]].

Between 1995 and 2007 Puerto Rico did not record a win, and had only four draws. During this time, the team dropped down in the [[FIFA World Rankings|FIFA Rankings]] to 202nd.<ref name="rsssf.com" />

=== 2000s ===
Puerto Rico began the new century by losing to [[Aruba national football team|Aruba]] in the first round of the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification|2002 World Cup qualifiers]] (6-4 on aggregate). Four years later, they withdrew from the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifiers]] for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]], allowing [[Belize national football team|Belize]] to advance in their place.

However, thanks to the participation of players in [[Major League Soccer|MLS]] and [[United Soccer League|USL]], the Puerto Rican national team significantly improved its level.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Libre |first=Diario |date=2010-01-08 |title=Miguel Torres en figureo |url=https://www.diariolibre.com/fotos/miguel-torres-en-figureo-HGDLGAL1237 |access-date=2026-02-09 |website=Diario Libre |language=es}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Bocanegra |first=Eyl |date=2026-02-06 |title="Estoy sorprendido por el nivel de Puerto Rico" Rueda |url=https://hondurasfutbol.com/estoy-sorprendido-por-el-nivel-de-puerto-rico-rueda/ |access-date=2026-02-09 |language=es}}</ref> In 2008, they won two games as visitors against Bermuda (1–0 and 2–0), and drew 2–2 with [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]]. They also won for the first time since 1994 when they beat the [[Dominican Republic national football team|Dominican Republic]] in the first round of the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] [[FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifiers]], although they lost the first leg 4–0 to [[Honduras national football team|Honduras]], they fought a 2–2 tie in the home leg in the second round, and were therefore eliminated from contention.

The [[Puerto Rican Football Federation]] then announced plans that would have allowed them to participate in the next editions of the [[Caribbean Cup]] and [[CONCACAF Gold Cup]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.primerahora.com/diario/noticia/futbol/accion_deportiva/con_la_mira_en_la_copa_de_oro_2011/318405 |title=Primerahora.com |website=primerahora.com |publisher=GFR Media LLC |access-date=June 30, 2010 |archive-date=August 10, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180810011458/http://www.primerahora.com/diario/noticia/futbol/accion_deportiva/con_la_mira_en_la_copa_de_oro_2011/318405 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

Puerto Rico was supposed to make their debut in the [[Football at the 2010 Central American and Caribbean Games|2010 Central American and Caribbean Games]] on home soil in [[Mayagüez, Puerto Rico|Mayagüez]], but due to the controversy of [[CONCACAF]] not approving the stadiums, the team couldn't compete in the football event. The team was supposed to play in [[Venezuela]] instead but due to the lack of teams, the male event was canceled.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.meta.pr/content/futbol-femenino-arranca-manana-en-venezuela |title=Fútbol femenino arranca mañana en Venezuela &#124; META MAYAGUEZ 2010 |access-date=August 2, 2010|url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110708053346/http://www.meta.pr/content/futbol-femenino-arranca-manana-en-venezuela |archive-date=July 8, 2011 }}</ref>

=== 2010s ===
In the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification|2014 World Cup qualifiers]], Puerto Rico was placed in Group D of the first round, along with [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada]], Saint Kitts and Nevis, and [[Saint Lucia national football team|Saint Lucia]]. They finished second in the group behind Canada, with 9 points. Although that total was insufficient to advance to the second round, the Blue Hurricane earned a commendable 0-0 draw in [[Toronto]] on October 11, 2011.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-10-12 |title=Selección queda fuera de la eliminatoria mundialista |url=https://www.primerahora.com/deportes/futbol/notas/seleccion-queda-fuera-de-la-eliminatoria-mundialista/ |access-date=2026-02-09 |website=Primera Hora |language=es}}</ref> Many of the players on the Puerto Rican national team also played for the [[Puerto Rico Islanders]], a team in the [[North American Soccer League (2011–2017)|NASL]], which at the time was the [[United States soccer league system|second division]] of American soccer.[[File:PUR vs Spain.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Puerto Rico (in blue) playing [[Spain national football team|Spain]] in 2012.]]
On August 15, 2012, Puerto Rico hosted [[Spain national football team|Spain]], the reigning [[FIFA World Cup|world]] and [[UEFA European Championship|European]] champions, in a prestigious international friendly played at the [[Juan Ramón Loubriel Stadium]] in the city of [[Bayamón, Puerto Rico|Bayamón]]. Although the Spanish dominated the match, they only managed a narrow 2-1 victory (goals by [[Santi Cazorla]] and [[Cesc Fàbregas]]; Cintrón scored for Puerto Rico). It was the first time Puerto Rico had played against a world champion team and also Spain's first encounter with a Caribbean nation. The Spanish press was highly critical of the match.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Critican duramente amistoso entre España y Puerto Rico |url=http://voces.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/16/critican-amistoso-espana-puerto-rico_n_1792697.html |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20160305013830/http://voces.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/16/critican-amistoso-espana-puerto-rico_n_1792697.html |archive-date=2016-03-05 |access-date=2026-02-09 |work=HuffPost Voces |language=es-LA}}</ref>

On June 5, 2015, Puerto Rico played a friendly against [[Bermuda national football team|Bermuda]], their last friendly before the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifiers]] for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup|2018 World Cup]] in [[Russia]]. Puerto Rico received a bye to the second round where they faced [[Grenada national football team|Grenada]]. In the first match, in Bayamón, they secured an important 1-0 victory, but in the return leg, they lost 2-0 in [[Grenada]], thus being eliminated from the World Cup with a 2-1 aggregate score. After losing to [[Grenada national football team|Grenada]] 2–1 in the second round of the 2018 World Cup qualifiers, Puerto Rico's Interim coach Jose 'Cukito' Martinez resigned and a few days later, the [[Uruguayan]] Carlos Avedissian solved his visa problems and could finally arrive as the head coach of the national team. On December 11, 2015, Puerto Rico played a friendly against [[Major League Soccer|MLS]] team [[New York City FC|New York City]], losing 2–1.

In the 2016 Caribbean Cup, Puerto Rico began their campaign by defeating [[Anguilla national football team|Anguilla]] 4-0 and losing to [[Guyana national football team|Guyana]] 1-0, which allowed them to advance to the second round. There, they achieved a 1-0 victory against [[Antigua and Barbuda national football team|Antigua and Barbuda]] and a draw against Grenada, whom they then defeated 4-3 in a penalty shootout. This marked Puerto Rico's historic achievement of reaching the Final Phase for the first time since 1993. In the final phase, they faced Antigua and Barbuda again, losing 3-0, and closed out the tournament with a 4-2 defeat against [[Curaçao national football team|Curaçao]].

==== Carlos Avedissian / Jack Stefanowski era ====
Uruguayan manager [[Garabet Avedissian|Carlos Avedissian]] took charge of the team in 2015 forming a roster mainly of players from the Puerto Rico local leagues. On May 22, 2016, Puerto Rico played a friendly match against the [[United States]] for the first time ever ending in a 3–1 loss.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2016/05/puerto-rico-cae-en-renido-amistoso-ante.html|title=Puerto Rico cae en reñido amistoso ante USA|first=Publicado por Javier|last=Torres|access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=September 27, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927185847/http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2016/05/puerto-rico-cae-en-renido-amistoso-ante.html|url-status=live}}</ref> For discrepancies with the Federation, Avedissian was substituted by Jack Stefanowski for the second round on an interim basis. With this team mainly of local players, Puerto Rico advanced for the first time to the third round of the [[2017 Caribbean Cup qualification]] when they defeated [[Grenada national football team|Grenada]] and [[Antigua and Barbuda national football team|Antigua and Barbuda]] in the second round, only two games away from reaching the [[2017 CONCACAF Gold Cup|CONCACAF Gold Cup]] for the first time. [[Jack Stefanowski|Stefanowski]] had to leave the technical director position because he was an assistant for Puerto Rico.{{citation needed|date=June 2020}}

On September 3, 2016, Puerto Rico faced [[India men's national football team|India]] for the first time in an international friendly match at the [[Mumbai Football Arena]], but were defeated in the contest 1–4.<ref>{{cite web|title=India 4–1 Puerto Rico|url=https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2016/09/03/world/friendlies/india/puerto-rico/2331720/|website=Soccerway.com|access-date=June 27, 2021|archive-date=August 26, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180826133408/https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2016/09/03/world/friendlies/india/puerto-rico/2331720/|url-status=live}}</ref>

==== Carlos García Cantarero era ====
After Stefanowski left the team to continue with Puerto Rico, [[Carlos García Cantarero|Carlos Cantarero]] assumed as head coach of the national team with Jose Cukito Martinez (who had coached most of the local players in the Puerto Rico League) as assistant coach. The team played three friendlies, two against the Dominican Republic and one against India, prior to the third round matches against Antigua and Barbuda and [[Curaçao national football team|Curaçao]]. Coach Cantarero couldn't coach in the friendlies due to visa problems. After the second friendly against the Dominican Republic Assistant Coach Martinez left the team prior to the trip to India and David Guillemat assumed the position. Regardless of the administrative debacle, and coaching changes, Puerto Rico lost the decisive match against Curaçao after being 2–0 up front for which Curaçao as head of group advanced to the Gold Cup.

Puerto Rico didn't play an international match until the summer of 2017 when they played to a scoreless draw against Indonesia.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2017/06/renido-empate-ante-indonesia.html|title=Reñido empate ante Indonesia|first=Publicado por Javier|last=Torres|access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=September 27, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927200342/http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2017/06/renido-empate-ante-indonesia.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

After the devastation of [[Hurricane Maria]] in September that left the entire island powerless. On October 7 it was reported [[Major League Soccer|MLS]] club, [[Orlando City SC]] announced a ''Fuerza Puerto Rico’ Friendly'' for November 4 with all the net proceeds from the match going towards the ''United for Puerto Rico'' initiative to raise funds and aid recovery efforts for the island. Reactivating the national team for a 3rd time this year.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://futbolboricua.co/la-seleccion-se-activa-en-beneficio-de-reconstruccion-de-puerto-rico/|title=La Selección se activa en beneficio de reconstrucción de Puerto Rico &#124; FutbolBoricua.net|date=October 7, 2017 |access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=December 1, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201201203324/https://futbolboricua.co/la-seleccion-se-activa-en-beneficio-de-reconstruccion-de-puerto-rico/|url-status=live}}</ref> The friendly marked Orlando City’s last game of 2017, as well team captain, [[Kaká]] with the lions.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://futbolboricua.co/kaka-jugara-su-ultimo-partido-contra-puerto-rico/|title=Kaká jugará su último partido contra Puerto Rico &#124; FutbolBoricua.net|date=October 12, 2017 |access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=April 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210414044225/https://futbolboricua.co/kaka-jugara-su-ultimo-partido-contra-puerto-rico/|url-status=live}}</ref> Puerto Rico lost the exhibition match 6–1.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2017/11/cae-puerto-rico-6-1-ante-orlando-city-sc.html|title=Cae Puerto Rico 6–1 ante Orlando City SC|first=Publicado por Javier|last=Torres|access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=September 22, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200922110523/http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2017/11/cae-puerto-rico-6-1-ante-orlando-city-sc.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

==== Amado Guevara era ====
In May 2018, the Puerto Rican Football Federation announced that Carlos Cantarero would no longer continue as head coach of the national team, with former Honduran player [[Amado Guevara]] taking over as head coach.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2018/05/fpf-anuncia-salida-de-los-tecnicos.html|title=FPF anuncia salida de los técnicos nacionales|first=Publicado por Javier|last=Torres|access-date=April 17, 2020|archive-date=September 27, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927200043/http://noticias.elhuracanazulpr.com/2018/05/fpf-anuncia-salida-de-los-tecnicos.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2018, the new [[CONCACAF Nations League]] was launched, featuring a qualification system to allocate the remaining nine spots for the [[2019 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2019 Gold Cup]] and simultaneously place the teams in the corresponding leagues based on their final standings after four matches. Puerto Rico finished second to last in the standings, occupying 33rd place out of 34 participants, earning no points in their four matches, losing them all (1-0 vs. Saint Kitts and Nevis, Belize, and Martinique; 2-0 vs. Grenada). This placed them in League C, preventing them from qualifying for the Gold Cup.

In 2019, during the Nations League group stage, Puerto Rico was placed in Group C alongside Guatemala and Anguilla. The team performed acceptably, winning both matches against Anguilla (3-2 and 3-0, respectively) and losing both games to Guatemala (5-0).

=== 2020s ===
[[File:Zarek Valentin Timbers (cropped).jpg|thumb|150px|right|[[Zarek Valentin]] made his debut for Puerto Rico in 2021]]

The decade started well for Puerto Rico, where they played two friendly matches in January 2021 against the Dominican Republic, where they achieved a historic 1-0 victory in Santo Domingo and finally against Guatemala, where they lost 1-0.<ref>{{Cite web |last=ELNUEVODIA.COM |first=Por |date=2021-01-23 |title=Puerto Rico cae 1-0 ante Guatemala en otro partido amistoso |url=https://www.elnuevodia.com/deportes/futbol/notas/puerto-rico-cae-1-0-ante-guatemala-en-otro-partido-amistoso/ |access-date=2026-02-09 |website=El Nuevo Día |language=spanish}}</ref>

However, the team would once again fail to qualify for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup|World Cup in 2022]]. In the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifiers]] for this edition, the team lost away to [[Saint Kitts and Nevis national football team|Saint Kitts and Nevis]] 1-0 (in a game played in [[San Cristóbal, Dominican Republic|San Cristóbal]] in the [[Dominican Republic]]), tied with [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]] at home 1-1, thrashed the [[Bahamas national football team|Bahamas]] 7-0 at home (both home games were played at the [[Mayagüez Athletics Stadium|Athletics Stadium]] [[Mayagüez, Puerto Rico|Mayagüez]]), before defeating [[Guyana national football team|Guyana]] 2-0 away (in a match played in [[Saint Kitts and Nevis]]). This wasn't enough for Puerto Rico, who was knocked out in the first round of qualification after finishing third in their group behind Trinidad and Tobago and group winners Saint Kitts and Nevis.

On March 19, 2023, just days before the Nations League, the Puerto Rican Football Federation announced [[Charlie Trout]] as the new head coach of the national team.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://uicflames.com/news/2023/3/20/mens-soccer-uic-alum-trout-named-head-coach-of-puerto-rican-mens-national-soccer-team.aspx | title=UIC Alum Trout Named Head Coach of Puerto Rican Men's National Soccer Team }}</ref>

==== 2022–23 Nations League ====
In the second season of the [[CONCACAF Nations League]], Puerto Rico would again be a part of the [[2022–23 CONCACAF Nations League C|C-League]]. This time being inserted to Group D alongside the [[British Virgin Islands national football team|British Virgin Islands]] and the [[Cayman Islands national football team|Cayman Islands]]. The team would go on to beat the British Islands 3–1<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.virginislandsdailynews.com/sports/puerto-rico-overhauls-bvi-in-concacaf-nations-league-finale/article_2705b008-b024-559c-9f6d-fc314fe090bd.html | title=Puerto Rico overhauls BVI in CONCACAF Nations League finale | date=March 24, 2023 }}</ref> and then later beat the Cayman Islands 5–1. The team would go on to win the group stage, thus earning a [[2023 CONCACAF Gold Cup qualification]] invitation and promotion into the B league for the next Nations league. 

In the CONCACAF Gold Cup preliminary, the team would draw against [[Suriname national football team|Suriname]] 0–0 and later winning in a penalty shootout 4–3.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.concacaf.com/gold-cup/article/puerto-rico-slip-past-suriname-in-dramatic-shootout/ | title=Puerto Rico slip past Suriname in dramatic shootout | date=June 14, 2023 |website=[[CONCACAF]] }}{{dead link |date=June 2026}}</ref> In the second round of the prelims, Puerto Rico would face [[Martinique national football team|Martinique]]. The team would lose 2–0 and be eliminated from the tournament.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.concacaf.com/gold-cup/article/martinique-fend-off-puerto-rico-to-punch-group-c-ticket/ | title=Martinique fend off Puerto Rico to punch Group C ticket | date=June 19, 2023 }}</ref>

==== 2026 World Cup qualification campaign ====
For the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification|2026 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], Puerto Rico received a bye to the second round. Their campaign began with a goalless away draw against [[El Salvador national football team|El Salvador]], followed by an 8–0 thrashing of [[Anguilla national football team|Anguilla]]. Puerto Rico then lost 1–0 away to [[Suriname national football team|Suriname]] before defeating [[Saint Vincent and the Grenadines national football team|Saint Vincent and the Grenadines]] 2–1 at home. Finishing third in their group behind Suriname and El Salvador, Puerto Rico failed to advance to the next stage.

==== 2026 FIFA Series ====
The [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|men's competition]] of the 2026 FIFA Series was split into nine different sections hosted across eight countries. In November 2025, FIFA announced that Puerto Rico would host one such section in [[Bayamón, Puerto Rico|Bayamón]], in which [[Guam national football team|Guam]], the [[United States Virgin Islands national soccer team|U.S. Virgin Islands]] and [[American Samoa national football team|American Samoa]] would also compete. The [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)#Puerto Rico|Puerto Rican section of the tournament]] was held in a four-match format (semi-finals, third-place match and final) in late March 2026. Puerto Rico were the section's champions after victories against Guam and the U.S. Virgin Islands in the semi-final and final, respectively.<ref name="2026 FIFA Series overview">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 3, 2026 |title=FIFA Series 2026: Everything you need to know |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/news/articles/fifa-series-everything-you-need-to-know |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 1, 2026}}</ref><ref name="2026 FIFA Series champions">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 29, 2026 |title=De Leon propels Puerto Rico to title |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/articles/puerto-rico-champions-guam-finish-third |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 1, 2026}}</ref>

=== Coaching history ===
:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Eduardo Ordóñez Munguira]] (1959)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Raúl Marchant González]] (1966)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} ''[[Egberto Morales Carrasco]]'' (1972)
* {{flagicon|Spain|1945}}/{{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Luis Villarejo]] (1974–1975)
* {{flagicon|Argentina}} [[Carlos Martinolli]] (1978–1979)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Joe Serralta]] (1979–1982)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Juan "Saso" Tullier]] (1982–1984)
* {{flagicon|Argentina}} [[Ricardo "Richie" Romano]] (1985–1990)
* {{flagicon|Brazil|1968}} [[Víctor Hugo Barros]] (1990–1991)
* {{flagicon|USA}} [[Arnie Ramirez]] (1992)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico|1952}} [[Jorge Oscar Rosa|Oscar Rosa]] (1992)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico}} [[Cris Vaccaro|Cristóbal Vaccaro]] (1996)
* {{flagicon|Spain}} [[José Luis "Majo" Rodríguez]] (1999)
* {{flagicon|Brazil}} [[Raimundo Gatinho]] (2000)
* {{flagicon|Costa Rica}} [[Toribio Rojas]] (2002–2003)
* {{flagicon|Brazil}} [[Víctor Hugo Barros]] (2004)
* {{flagicon|Northern Ireland}} [[Colin Clarke (footballer born 1962)|Colin Clarke]] (2007–2011)
* {{flagicon|USA}} ''[[Jack Stefanowski]]'' (2011)
* {{flagicon|England}} [[Adrian Whitbread]] (2011)
* {{flagicon|Costa Rica}} [[Jeaustin Campos]] (2011–2013)
* {{flagicon|Brazil}} [[Víctor Hugo Barros]] (2013–2014)
* {{flagicon|Puerto Rico}} ''[[José "Cukito" Martinez]]'' (2015)
* {{flagicon|Uruguay}} [[Garabet Avedissian|Carlos Avedissian]] (2015–2016)
* {{flagicon|USA}} ''[[Jack Stefanowski]]'' (2016)
* {{flagicon|Spain}} [[Carlos García Cantarero]] (2016–2018)
* {{flagicon|Honduras|1949}} [[Amado Guevara]] (2018–2019)
* {{flagicon|Costa Rica}} [[Elgy Morales]] (2019–2021)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/coaches/elgy-morales/294747/|title=Puerto Rico – E. Morales – Profile with news, career statistics and history – Soccerway|website=us.soccerway.com|access-date=February 22, 2020|archive-date=September 6, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190906114049/https://us.soccerway.com/coaches/elgy-morales/294747/|url-status=live}}</ref>
* {{flagicon|United States}} [[Dave Sarachan]] (2021–2022)
* {{flagicon|England}} [[Charlie Trout]] (2023–present)
{{div col end}}

== Players ==
=== Current squad ===
The following players were called up for the [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)]] matches against [[Guam national football team|Guam]] and either [[U.S. Virgin Islands national soccer team|U.S. Virgin Islands]] or [[American Samoa national football team|American Samoa]] on 25 and 28 March 2026, respectively.<ref>{{cite tweet|title=Estos son los convocados de Puerto Rico para la FIFA Series 2026|user=FPFPuertoRico|number=2033619406698267130||author=[[Puerto Rican Football Federation]]|access-date=16 March 2026|date=16 March 2026|language=es}}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of May 12, 2026''.

|-
|}

== Competitive record ==

|-

|{{fb|PUR}} 0–5 {{fb-rt|GUA}}<br>(September 10, 2019; [[Mayagüez, Puerto Rico|Mayagüez]], Puerto Rico)<br>{{fb|GUA}} 5–0 {{fb-rt|PUR}}<br>(November 16, 2019; [[Guatemala City]], Guatemala)
